section.title: The Righteousness of Faith
section.sectionRationale: Paul now moves from God's sovereign choice to Israel's own responsibility. The stunning result is that Gentiles who never pursued righteousness have obtained it by faith, while Israel, chasing a law of righteousness, failed to reach it, because they sought it by works and stumbled over the stumbling stone. The reason is not that God's word failed but that they were ignorant of God's righteousness and tried to establish their own. Paul then unveils the way of righteousness by faith: Christ is the end and goal of the law, the word of faith is near, and whoever confesses Jesus as Lord and believes God raised Him will be saved, for everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved, with no distinction between Jew and Greek.
section.thesis: Righteousness comes not by the works of the law but by faith in Christ, who is the end and goal of the law; the saving word is near, so that whoever confesses Jesus as Lord and believes in his heart that God raised Him will be saved, and this salvation is offered without distinction to everyone who calls on the name of the Lord.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the great reversal, Gentiles attaining righteousness by faith while Israel stumbled over the stone by seeking it through works; Christ the end of the law and Israel's misdirected zeal; and the word of faith, confess and believe and be saved, for everyone who calls on the Lord.

units.label: The great reversal and the stumbling stone
units.note: Gentiles attain righteousness by faith, Israel stumbles

verses.text: What then will we say? That the Gentiles, who did not pursue righteousness, have obtained it, a {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}} that is by faith;
but Israel, who pursued a law of righteousness, has not attained it.
Why not? Because their pursuit was not by faith, but as if it were by works. They stumbled over the stumbling stone,
as it is written: “See, I lay in Zion a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense; and the one who believes in Him will never be put to shame.”

units.label: Christ, the end of the law

verses.text: Brothers, my heart's desire and prayer to God for the Israelites is for their salvation.
For I testify about them that they are zealous for God, but not on the basis of knowledge.
Because they were ignorant of God's righteousness and sought to establish their own, they did not submit to God's righteousness.
For Christ is the {{telos|end}} of the law, to bring righteousness to everyone who believes.

units.label: The word of faith near you
units.note: confess and believe and be saved

verses.text: For concerning the righteousness that is by the law, Moses writes: “The man who does these things will live by them.”
But the righteousness that is by faith says: “Do not say in your heart, ‘Who will ascend into heaven?’ (that is, to bring Christ down)
or, ‘Who will descend into the Abyss?’ (that is, to bring Christ up from the dead).”
But what does it say? “The word is near you; it is in your mouth and in your heart,” that is, the word of faith we are proclaiming:
that if you {{homologeo|confess}} with your mouth, “Jesus is Lord,” and believe in your heart that God raised Him from the dead, you will be saved.
For with your heart you believe and are justified, and with your mouth you confess and are saved.
It is just as the Scripture says: “Anyone who believes in Him will never be put to shame.”
For there is no difference between Jew and Greek: The same {{kyrios|Lord}} is Lord of all, and gives richly to all who call on Him,
for, “Everyone who {{epikaleo|calls on}} the name of the Lord will be saved.”

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, the right standing God requires and gives. Paul contrasts a righteousness pursued by works, which Israel failed to reach, with the righteousness that is by faith, freely received. God's righteousness is not a standard we climb to but a gift bestowed on all who believe, credited apart from the works of the law.

telos.gloss: End, goal, or fulfillment. When Paul says Christ is the telos of the law, he means both that Christ is the terminus of the law as a way of earning righteousness and the goal toward which the law always pointed. In Him the law reaches its intended purpose, bringing righteousness to everyone who believes.

homologeo.gloss: To confess, to say the same thing, to acknowledge openly. To confess that Jesus is Lord is to agree with God's verdict about His Son and to own Him publicly. This confession, joined to heartfelt belief in the resurrection, is the outward voice of a faith that receives salvation.

kyrios.gloss: Lord, master, sovereign. In the Greek Old Testament kyrios rendered the divine name, so to confess Jesus as Lord is to acknowledge His divine sovereignty and reign. Paul says the same Lord is Lord of all, rich to all who call on Him, applying to Jesus the name and honor of the LORD Himself.

epikaleo.gloss: To call upon, to invoke, to appeal to. Quoting Joel, Paul says everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved. To call on the Lord is to turn to Him in faith and cast oneself on His mercy, and this door stands open to all without distinction, Jew and Greek alike.

basic.context: Paul turns from God's sovereign choice in chapter nine to Israel's responsibility. He faces a startling outcome: Gentiles who never sought righteousness have received it by faith, while Israel, zealously pursuing a law of righteousness, failed to attain it. Why? Because they sought it by works rather than faith and stumbled over Christ, the stumbling stone. Paul aches for their salvation, names their sincere but misdirected zeal, and then lays out the way of righteousness by faith in Christ.
basic.hermeneutics: Read this as the flip side of chapter nine. There Paul defended God's freedom; here he presses Israel's real fault. The two truths stand together: God is sovereign, and people are responsible. The heart of the passage is the contrast between establishing your own righteousness and submitting to God's righteousness, received by simple faith in the crucified and risen Christ, whose saving word is near.
basic.originalLanguages: The theme word is dikaiosyne, righteousness, either pursued by works and missed, or received by faith. Christ is the telos of the law, its end and goal. Salvation comes as we homologeo, confess, Jesus as kyrios, Lord, the divine name applied to Him. And it is for everyone who epikaleo, calls on, the name of the Lord.
basic.typology: The stumbling stone laid in Zion, from Isaiah, is fulfilled in Christ, who is both a rock of offense to the unbelieving and a sure foundation to those who trust Him. Moses' words about the nearness of the word, from Deuteronomy, are fulfilled as the gospel of Christ comes near to every heart and mouth.
basic.christ: Christ is the center: He is the stumbling stone over which Israel fell, yet the one in whom no believer is put to shame. He is the end and goal of the law, bringing righteousness to all who believe. He is the Lord to be confessed, raised from the dead, and the same Lord of all, rich to everyone who calls on Him.
basic.god: God is righteous and gives righteousness as a gift. Israel's tragedy was ignoring God's righteousness to build their own. God lays the stone in Zion, brings the word near, justifies the believing heart, and saves all who call, showing no partiality between Jew and Greek. His salvation is generous, near, and free.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 28 and 8 speak of the stone in Zion. Leviticus 18 gives the law's do this and live. Deuteronomy 30 speaks of the word near you. Joel 2 promises salvation to all who call on the name of the LORD.
basic.oneStory: The Bible's long question of how sinners can be righteous before God comes to its clearest answer here. Not by works of the law, at which Israel labored and failed, but by faith in Christ, the end and goal of the law. The saving word is near, and the door stands open to all peoples, so that everyone who calls on the Lord, Jew or Gentile, will be saved.

context.literary: This passage spans the chapter boundary, running from the conclusion of chapter nine, verses 30 to 33, into the opening of chapter ten, verses 1 to 13. It is a single argument: the diagnosis of Israel's failure and the Gentiles' faith, then the remedy in Christ the end of the law and the word of faith. The stumbling stone of 9:33 and the never put to shame of 10:11 frame the section around Isaiah's promise.
context.historical: Paul writes as one who had himself been a zealous Jew, blameless in law keeping, and understands from within the sincere zeal that is not according to knowledge. To a mixed church he explains the paradox of his own day: Gentiles streaming in by faith while many Jews resist, not because the gospel is narrow but because they will not submit to God's righteousness in Christ.
context.canonical: Romans 10 develops the theme of righteousness by faith first stated in 1:16-17 and expounded in chapters three and four, now applied to Israel. Its citation of Deuteronomy 30 and Joel 2, and its confession Jesus is Lord, became foundational for the church's understanding of saving faith and its open invitation to all nations.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To explain why Israel failed to attain righteousness while Gentiles received it, locating the cause in seeking righteousness by works rather than faith, and to set forth clearly the way of salvation by faith in Christ, the end of the law, freely offered to all who confess Him as Lord and call on His name.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul describes the actual state of Israel and the Gentiles in his day, but his teaching on the righteousness of faith is prescriptive for all: righteousness is received, not achieved; salvation comes by confessing Jesus as Lord and believing in the resurrection; and the invitation is open to everyone who calls on the Lord.

debates.question: What does it mean that Christ is the end of the law?

views.label: Termination as a way of righteousness
views.case: Telos can mean end or termination: Christ brings to a close the law as a means of achieving righteousness, so that pursuing right standing by works of the law is over for those who believe. Israel stumbled precisely by treating the law as if it were by works.

views.label: Goal and fulfillment
views.case: Telos can also mean goal or aim: Christ is the destination toward which the law always pointed, its intended fulfillment, so that the law finds its true meaning in Him. He does not abolish its witness but consummates it, bringing righteousness to all who believe.

views.label: Both together
views.case: Many read telos as deliberately holding both senses: Christ is the end of the law as a ladder of self-righteousness and the goal to which it always pointed. The two meanings reinforce one another and fit Paul's argument that righteousness is now by faith in Christ.

debates.bearing: Whether one stresses termination, goal, or both, the saving point is the same and is foregrounded here: righteousness is no longer sought by law keeping but received by faith in Christ, in whom the law reaches its purpose, so that righteousness comes to everyone who believes.

debates.question: Does confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord teach that verbal confession is a separate condition added to faith?

views.label: Confession as the voice of faith
views.case: The historic reading holds that heart faith and mouth confession are two aspects of one saving response, not two separate conditions. Verse 10 pairs them poetically, believing unto righteousness and confessing unto salvation; genuine faith naturally owns Christ openly, and Paul is not adding a work to faith.

views.label: Confession as a distinct requirement
views.case: Some press the two clauses to mean that public confession is a separate act required alongside believing; this can slide toward making the confession a work, which the surrounding emphasis on faith and God's free gift resists.

debates.bearing: The passage upholds salvation by faith received and openly owned: true faith in the risen Lord confesses Him, so that confession is faith finding its voice, not a meritorious addition, and the whole rests on grace, for everyone who calls on the Lord will be saved.

study.originalLanguages: The passage turns on dikaiosyne, righteousness, set in sharp contrast: a righteousness Israel chased by works and failed to reach, and the righteousness that is by faith, freely given to all who believe. Christ is the telos of the law, at once its end as a way of earning acceptance and the goal to which it always pointed. The saving response is to homologeo, to confess openly, that Jesus is kyrios, Lord, the very name of the LORD now owned as His, joined to heartfelt belief that God raised Him. And the promise reaches everyone who will epikaleo, call upon, the name of the Lord, without distinction between Jew and Greek.

typology.fulfillment: Isaiah's stone of stumbling and rock of offense is fulfilled in Christ, who trips the self-righteous but is the sure foundation on whom no believer is put to shame.
Moses' word about the nearness of the commandment is fulfilled as the word of faith, the gospel of Christ, comes near to every heart and mouth for salvation.

typology.type: Everyone who calls on the name of the LORD
typology.fulfillment: Joel's promise to all who call on the LORD is fulfilled in Christ, on whom Jew and Gentile alike now call and are saved.

study.christ: Christ stands at the heart of this passage in every part. He is the stumbling stone laid in Zion, over which Israel fell by unbelief, yet the very one in whom the believer will never be put to shame. He is the end and goal of the law, so that all striving to earn righteousness ends in Him and finds its aim in Him, bringing righteousness to everyone who believes. He is the Lord to be confessed, raised from the dead by God, and the same Lord of all, rich in mercy to everyone who calls on Him. To submit to God's righteousness is simply to trust and confess Christ.
study.god: God is the giver of righteousness and the author of salvation. Israel's tragedy was to be ignorant of God's righteousness and to try to establish their own, refusing to submit to His gift. God lays the stone in Zion, brings His word near, justifies the believing heart, and saves all who confess and call. He shows no partiality, being the same Lord of all, generous to Jew and Greek alike. His salvation is near, free, and wide open, magnifying His grace and impartial mercy.

crossReferences.note: The precious stone laid in Zion; the one who believes will never be put to shame, applied by Paul to Christ.
The man who does these things will live by them, the principle of the righteousness that is by the law.
The word is near you, in your mouth and heart, which Paul applies to the word of faith in Christ.
Everyone who calls on the name of the LORD will be saved, fulfilled in the universal offer of the gospel.

study.oneStory: The whole Bible presses the question of how a sinner can be righteous before God. Israel labored to answer it by the works of the law and stumbled over the very stone God laid in Zion. Paul shows the true answer: righteousness by faith in Christ, the end and goal of the law. The saving word has come near, so that with the heart one believes and with the mouth confesses Jesus as Lord, and the ancient promise is fulfilled, that everyone who calls on the name of the Lord, Jew or Gentile, will be saved.

quiz.q: Why did Israel fail to attain the righteousness they pursued?

options.text: Because they pursued it not by faith but as if it were by works, and stumbled over the stumbling stone, Christ.
options.note: Verses 32 and 33 name the cause: seeking righteousness by works and stumbling over Christ.

options.text: Because they did not try hard enough to keep the law.
options.note: Their zeal was real; the fault was the wrong method, works instead of faith.

options.text: Because God gave them no opportunity to believe.
options.note: God laid the stone and brought the word near; the fault was unbelief, not lack of opportunity.

options.text: Because the law itself was evil.
options.note: The law is not evil; Israel misused it as a way of works rather than pointing to Christ.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean that Christ is the end of the law?

options.text: Christ is the end and goal of the law, so that righteousness now comes to everyone who believes, not by law keeping.
options.note: Verse 4: Christ terminates the law as a way of earning righteousness and fulfills its aim.

options.text: Christ made the moral distinctions of right and wrong meaningless.
options.note: Christ does not abolish righteousness; He brings it to all who believe.

options.text: Christ merely reformed the law into stricter rules.
options.note: The point is not stricter rules but righteousness by faith in Him.

options.text: Christ is the goal the law pointed to, though it can still justify by works.
options.note: He is the goal, but Paul denies that the law can justify by works at all.

quiz.q: According to verse 9, how is a person saved?

options.text: By confessing with the mouth that Jesus is Lord and believing in the heart that God raised Him from the dead.
options.note: Verse 9 joins heartfelt faith in the resurrection with the confession of Jesus as Lord.

options.text: By reciting the right words regardless of what the heart believes.
options.note: Confession must be joined to heart belief; it is faith finding its voice, not mere words.

options.text: By keeping the law well enough to earn God's approval.
options.note: Paul contrasts this saving faith with the failed pursuit of righteousness by works.

options.text: By being born a Jew and observing the covenant signs.
options.note: Paul says there is no difference between Jew and Greek; salvation is by faith.

quiz.q: What does it mean to confess that Jesus is Lord?

options.text: To acknowledge His divine sovereignty, applying to Jesus the name and honor of the LORD Himself.
options.note: Kyrios rendered the divine name; the same Lord is Lord of all, rich to all who call.

options.text: To treat Jesus as one good teacher among many.
options.note: The confession names Him Lord of all, not merely a teacher.

options.text: To recognize Him only as a human master.
options.note: Kyrios here carries the weight of the divine name, not merely an earthly master.

options.text: To submit to Him as a political ruler.
options.note: His lordship is divine and saving, not merely political.

quiz.q: Who may be saved according to verses 12 and 13?

options.text: Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord, with no difference between Jew and Greek.
options.note: The same Lord is Lord of all, rich to all who call, quoting Joel's open promise.

options.text: Only those descended from Abraham.
options.note: Paul says there is no difference between Jew and Greek.

options.text: Only those who first keep the whole law.
options.note: Salvation comes by calling on the Lord in faith, not by law keeping.

options.text: Only a select few chosen at random.
options.note: The invitation is genuinely open to everyone who calls, though salvation rests on God's mercy.

questions: Israel was zealous for God but not according to knowledge, and built their own righteousness. Where might your religious effort be an attempt to establish your own righteousness rather than submitting to God's?
Christ is the end and goal of the law. How does resting in Him as the finish of all your striving change the way you relate to God's commands?
The word of faith is near you, in your mouth and heart. How does the nearness and simplicity of the gospel comfort you when you are tempted to think salvation is out of reach?
Salvation comes by confessing Jesus as Lord and believing He is risen. Is there an area of your life where confessing Jesus as Lord openly would cost you something this week?
Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved, with no distinction. How does the width of this promise shape the way you pray for and speak to people very different from you?

translationNotes.note: End of the law renders telos, which can mean termination or goal. Christ is both the end of the law as a way to earn righteousness and the goal to which it pointed; the two senses reinforce one another.

Jesus is Lord renders the confession Kyrios Iesous. Since kyrios translated the divine name in the Greek Old Testament, the confession ascribes divine sovereignty to Jesus, matching the same Lord is Lord of all in verse 12.

Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved quotes Joel 2:32, where the name is the LORD; Paul applies it to Jesus, a striking witness to His deity and to the universal offer of the gospel.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Romans 9:30-33 and 10:1-13, reproduced contiguously across the chapter boundary. No em dashes required substitution in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
